content.full_text:
21 January 1992

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Re: Councilmember Harold Sponsler

Honorable Commissioners:

Harold Sponsler, a member of the Mt. Shasta City Council has asked me to submit this request for an opinion from the Fair Political Practices Commission, on his behalf.

Mr. Sponsler operates a nursery and florist business, and has made sales in excess of $250.00 to J & A Food Service, Inc., the owner/operator of the local "Burger King" restaurant during the past 12 months.

That restaurant was constructed on a parcel which was created by a subdivision approved by the City Council a couple of years ago. The restaurant building design was concurrently approved under the City's architectural ordinance. Those approvals were followed by a lawsuit against the City and J & A by a local group.

A written settlement agreement to the lawsuit provides for modifications to the original subdivision configuration, building design, parking and driveway areas, and other subjects, some of which were not part of the project originally submitted to, and approved by, the Council.

One subject, which was not part of the application of J & A Food Service, Inc., for its restaurant, nor of the subdivider of the parcel, but which is included in the settlement agreement, is that the City will construct a median strip on the block of the street in front of the Burger King restaurant. One result of that strip would be to prevent left-turns into and from one of the Burger King driveways, for safety reasons. The subject street median strip is included in the City's General Plan and construction is partially funded in its budget.

A subsequent opinion from a traffic engineer indicates that the subject street median strip could create a dangerous condition. Therefore, the Council is considering appropriate amendments to its budget, General Plan, etc., to delete the strip from its construction plans.

FPPC / 1-21-92 / P.2 / Mt. Shasta Median Strip

Councilmember Sponsler has been warned by proponents of the median strip that his financial dealings with the restaurant owner disqualify him from voting on any changes to the City's plans. He contends that is not the case, for he sees no direct financial effect on the restaurant, and requests an opinion from the FPPC.

Thank you for your assistance.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Robert D. Winston

RDW/KM
cc: Councilmember Sponsler
